summary: Jeff Atwood critiques UML (Universal Modeling Language) as a fundamentally flawed documentation standard despite its widespread adoption. Drawing from his experience working with offshore vendors, he argues that UML's imprecise semantics make it subjective—a fatal flaw for something meant to describe the precision of code. He highlights three core failures: lack of bidirectional synchronization with code, inability to capture important details like properties or static members, and no real advantage over plain text documentation. Atwood sides with The Pragmatic Programmer's advice to use whatever communication method works best rather than slavishly following UML notation.
key_points: UML's imprecise semantics cause subjective interpretation, which contradicts programming's inherent precision
UML violates DRY principles because diagrams and code don't stay synchronized automatically
UML cannot represent important programming concepts like Properties or Static members, forcing developers to read code anyway
Even Martin Fowler, a UML proponent, positions it mainly as informal whiteboard sketching rather than formal documentation
Plain text requirements documents are often more readable and useful than UML's lines, arrows, and stick figures
money_quotes: The last time I checked, programming was about the least subjective human activity on earth. So there's a huge disconnect.
If the UML changes, the code doesn't magically write itself. And if the code changes, the UML documents don't magically update themselves either.
I find it much easier to read a typical all-text requirements document than a mish-mash of lines, arrows, and primitive looking stick figures.
It seems incredible to us that anyone would seriously consider documenting information this dense using only simplistic stick people.
Don't be a slave to any notation; use whatever method best communicates the requirements with your audience.
UML can't capture many of the high-level details necessary to build software.
key_insight: Documentation tools should serve clear communication, not become rigid standards that add complexity without solving the fundamental problem of keeping specifications synchronized with code.
